chunk_summary: Guidance on selecting pathways for managing refeeding syndrome in under-5 SAM cases versus high-risk refeeding in older children with GI disease, emphasizing tailored calorie starts, electrolyte monitoring, and thiamine supplementation.
learning_objectives: Understand the differences between under-5 SAM protocols and high-risk refeeding syndrome management.
Learn the importance of cautious calorie initiation and electrolyte monitoring in refeeding syndrome.
Identify mixed-case scenarios requiring combined approaches in pediatric GI disease and malnutrition.
key_facts: Under-5 SAM protocols use structured WHO/NHM pathways with stabilization feeds followed by catch-up feeds.
Avoid adult-style calorie starts (e.g., 10 kcal/kg/day) in stabilized under-5 SAM cases.
High-risk refeeding syndrome management includes conservative calorie starts, thiamine supplementation, and close electrolyte monitoring for 72 hours.
Baseline low phosphate, potassium, or magnesium increases refeeding risk in older children and adolescents.
Mixed cases (e.g., older child with IBD and edema) require staged feeding plans with explicit phosphate monitoring and higher-acuity settings.
reference_quote: In children 6–59 months meeting SAM criteria, use the structured WHO/NHM inpatient SAM pathway because it was designed around pediatric physiology and includes built-in safeguards.
document_key_topics: High-risk refeeding syndrome in older children
Mixed malnutrition cases in pediatric GI disease
